data.title: Ovintiv GAAP EPS of $1.62 misses by $0.49
data.description:
• Ovintiv press release (OVV): Q2 GAAP EPS of $1.62 misses by $0.49.
  • Recognized a net gain on risk management in revenues of $122 million, before tax
  • Generated cash from operating activities of $1.6 billion and Non-GAAP Cash Flow of approximately $1.3 billion
  • Second quarter average total production volumes were approximately 615 MBOE/d, including 206 Mbbls/d of oil and condensate, 82 Mbbls/d of other NGLs (C2 to C4) and 1,959 MMcf/d of natural gas
  • Second quarter capital investment of $574 million was at the midpoint of the guidance range of $550 million to $600 million
  • Reported second quarter upstream operating expense of $3.25 per BOE, upstream transportation and processing costs of $9.47 per BOE, production, mineral and other taxes of $1.43 per BOE, or 3.5% of upstream product revenue
  • Excluding the impact of hedges, second quarter average realized price for oil and condensate was $97.50 per barrel (105% of WTI), $21.67 per barrel for other NGLs, and $1.71 per Mcf (59% of NYMEX) for natural gas, resulting in a total average realized price of $41.00 per BOE
  • Including the impact of hedges, second quarter average realized price for oil and condensate was $91.22 per barrel (98% of WTI), $21.67 per barrel for other NGLs, and $1.99 per Mcf (69% of NYMEX) for natural gas, resulting in a total average realized price of $39.79 per BOE

  • 2026 Guidance

    3Q 2026

    Full Year 2026

    Total Production (MBOE/d)

    615 – 640

    630 – 645

    Oil & Condensate (Mbbls/d) 

    205 – 210

    210 – 212

    NGLs (C2 to C4) (Mbbls/d)

    75 – 80

    83 – 85

    Natural Gas (MMcf/d)

    2,000 – 2,100

    2,025 – 2,075

    Capital Investment ($ Millions)

    $550 – $600

    $2,250 – $2,350

data.title: CNB Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $0.63, revenue of $87.6M
data.description:
• CNB Financial press release (CCNE): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.63.
  • Revenue of $87.6M.
  • As of June 30, 2026, the Corporation’s total shareholders’ equity was $909.4 million, representing an increase of $20.3 million, or 2.29%, from March 31, 2026, and an increase of $272.2 million, or 42.71%, from June 30, 2025. The quarter-over-quarter increase was primarily driven by growth in retained earnings of $21.6 million, net of the payment of common and preferred stock dividends, partially offset by a $2.3 million increase in accumulated other comprehensive loss. The year-over-year increase was primarily driven by a $206.1 million increase in additional paid-in capital related to the ESSA acquisition, growth in retained earnings of $69.9 million, net of the payment of common and preferred stock dividends, partially offset by a $3.1 million increase in accumulated other comprehensive loss, during the twelve months ended June 30, 2026.

data.title: Rheinmetall breaks ground on major German plant to expand ammunition output
data.description:
Rheinmetall (RNMBF)(RNMBY) said Thursday it begun construction of one of Europe's largest propellant powder plants, expanding ammunition production capacity in Germany as European countries accelerate defense spending.

The new facility will be built at the Aschau am Inn site in Bavaria, home to Rheinmetall subsidiary Nitrochemie Aschau. Production is scheduled to begin in 2027, with full capacity expected in 2028.

 

 

The expansion comes as European governments seek to replenish weapons stockpiles depleted by military aid to Ukraine following Russia's invasion. NATO members have also increased defense budgets after President Donald Trump renewed pressure on freeloading European allies to shoulder a larger share of the alliance's defense spending, fueling demand for ammunition and the industrial capacity needed to produce it.

Rheinmetall (RNMBF)(RNMBY) said it will invest several hundred million euros in the project. The company expects the site to expand from 90 hectares to about 110 hectares and create several hundred additional jobs, increasing total employment to about 1,400.

The Aschau facility currently produces about 1,700 metric tons of propellant powder and 300,000 propellant charge modules each year. Over the next 24 months, Rheinmetall said production will more than double, adding about 2,500 metric tons of powder, more than 5 million combustible components and more than 1 million modular propellant charge modules annually.

Propellant powder is a critical component of artillery ammunition and other large-caliber weapons. It generates the gas pressure that propels a projectile from a gun barrel.

The project forms part of Rheinmetall's Firepower initiative, which aims to expand ammunition manufacturing capacity across Europe. By 2030, the company plans to increase annual propellant powder production to 20,000 metric tons, with about 4,200 metric tons expected to come from the Aschau site.

Rheinmetall said it plans to invest about €650 million in propellant powder production and related precursor materials, including about €350 million at Aschau. The expansion also includes production facilities in Switzerland and Spain.

The company said the Aschau site will manage the full production chain for modular propellant charges, from raw materials through finished components.

data.title: SpaceX delays Starship flight test to Friday as weather hampers heat shield imaging
data.description:
SpaceX (SPCX) postponed the 13th flight test of its Starship rocket to Friday, July 24, after weather conditions threatened one of the mission's key objectives: capturing detailed ground-based images of the vehicle's heat shield during ascent.

The company announced the delay Thursday in a post on X, saying poor weather would prevent engineers from obtaining clear imagery of Starship's thermal protection system as the rocket passes through a period of high dynamic pressure shortly after liftoff.

"Now targeting Friday, July 24 for Starship's thirteenth flight test, due to weather," SpaceX (SPCX) said. "A key objective for the flight test is to get clear imagery from the ground of Starship's heatshield as it flies at a higher dynamic pressure during ascent, which won't be possible with today's."

For investors, Starship is more than an experimental rocket. It sits at the center of SpaceX's (SPCX) long-term strategy to dramatically increase launch capacity while lowering the cost of reaching orbit. The company has invested more than $15 billion in developing the vehicle and expanding infrastructure at its Starbase launch site, describing Starship as critical to deploying larger Starlink satellite constellations, expanding satellite-to-mobile services and eventually supporting orbital AI computing infrastructure.

First launched in 2023, Starship is designed to become the world's first fully reusable super heavy-lift launch vehicle. SpaceX (SPCX) says the next-generation Starship V3 is intended to carry up to 100 metric tons into Earth orbit while enabling aircraft-like turnaround times through rapid refurbishment and reuse. Future versions are expected to double that payload capacity.

The company has already demonstrated the ability to recover and catch Starship's Super Heavy booster using giant mechanical arms, known as "chopsticks," on the launch tower. SpaceX (SPCX) believes that capability will eventually support multiple launches per day while reducing launch costs by as much as 99% compared with historical industry averages.

Starship is also expected to begin delivering commercial payloads to orbit during the second half of 2026, making progress on the test program increasingly important to the company's broader commercial ambitions.

At the same time, SpaceX (SPCX) has cautioned investors that unexpected design changes, supply chain disruptions, technical anomalies, environmental issues and other engineering challenges could delay Starship's development and, in turn, slow the deployment of next-generation satellites and other space-based services.

Thursday's postponement illustrates the emphasis SpaceX (SPCX) places on collecting engineering data during test flights. While weather posed no fundamental obstacle to launching the vehicle, it would have prevented the company from obtaining the high-quality imagery needed to evaluate how Starship's heat shield performs under the intense aerodynamic forces experienced during ascent.

data.title: RenaissanceRe anticipates Q3 management fees around $50M while continuing share repurchases into wind season
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. (RNR) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "For the second quarter, we reported operating income of $548 million and an annualized operating return on equity of 20%. Tangible book value per share grew approximately 6% in the quarter and 27% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Robert O'Donnell)
  • "Property catastrophe rates were down high teens, which was consistent with our expectations" and "our leadership position allowed us to grow property-cat limit with high-quality clients." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • "This quarter, we repurchased $350 million of our shares at valuations rapidly accretive to tangible book value per share." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • "Where uncertainty remains and in casualty, it does, we remain cautious. Social inflation continues to impact casualty, and we have been proactive in recognizing trend over the last several years." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • "This is another strong quarter where we generated operating earnings per share of $12.92" and "an annualized operating return on equity of 20.1%." (Executive VP & CFO Robert Qutub)
  • "We reported strong accident year results with a low level of catastrophe activity and 9 percentage points of favorable development." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "We chose not to deploy our collateralized vehicle, Upsilon, at the midyear renewal, instead renewing the business on wholly-owned balance sheets." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "At the midyear renewal, we successfully grew U.S. property cat limit by $600 million." (Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer David Marra)
  • "Excluding the Baltimore Bridge and purchase accounting adjustments, prior year development for the segment overall would have been modestly favorable with an adjusted combined ratio in the high 90s, consistent with our guidance." (Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra)
  • "In 2027, Matt Nuber will become our Chief Financial Officer" and "we have previously announced Bob will retire at year-end." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead for the third quarter, we expect other property net premiums earned of around $330 million and an attritional loss ratio in the mid-50s." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Casualty and Specialty net premiums earned of approximately $1.3 billion, and adjusted combined ratio in the high 90s." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "In the third quarter, we expect management fees of around $50 million." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Performance fees are highly dependent on underwriting results, but should average around $30 million per quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "For the third quarter, we expect retained net investment income to continue to trend modestly higher." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • Compared with last quarter’s outlook language, management framed midyear property-cat pricing as "down high teens" in Q2 versus "down low teen percentages" discussed around January renewals, while maintaining the same emphasis that rates "remain broadly adequate" and that the company will use "more of the tools that are available to us" as competition builds. (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)

Financial Results

  • "Overall, gross premiums written were $3 billion, down 12%." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Our portfolio continues to perform well with an adjusted combined ratio of 72%." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "In property catastrophe, the current accident year loss ratio was 12% and the adjusted combined ratio was 9%." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "In Casualty and Specialty, the current accident year loss ratio was 67%, 68%, and the adjusted combined ratio was 102%." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "This was a result of a shift in reserves from other property to Specialty" and "the overall impact to the company was an increase in net negative impact related to the bridge of only $12 million in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Fee income... we generated $83 million in fees, including management fees of $48 million and performance fees of $35 million." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Retained net investment income was $314 million, up 3% from the first quarter or 10% from a year ago. This is an all-time high." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)
  • "Our operating expense ratio was 4.3%" and "we continue to invest in the business and expect the expense ratio to build towards 5% as the year progresses." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: asked how property-cat pricing may evolve without major hurricane losses; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "I would expect that... there's a lot of supply in the market" and "I would expect that there'll be more rate pressure."
  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: pressed on the Casualty & Specialty premium decline and reserve comfort; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "we have made some portfolio shaping decisions, mainly in the general liability space" and "ceded is something we have used for years... we see more opportunities to cede risk at attractive terms."
  • Joshua Shanker, BofA Securities: asked whether the credit premium decline was timing or lost share; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "a lot of the transactions are multiyear" and "our credit book is essentially flat."
  • Joshua Shanker, BofA Securities: challenged the economics of higher cessions with a ~100% combined ratio; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "We get an override" and "we also get reduced volatility."
  • Yaron Kinar, Mizuho: asked about other property growth; Executive VP & CFO Qutub: "it's about flat" on underlying risk.
  • Yaron Kinar, Mizuho: asked about buyback pace; Executive VP & CFO Qutub: "we're still focused on buying through the wind season, absent any large events that occur."
  • Meyer Shields, KBW: asked what actuarial views drive higher casualty cessions; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "Right now, there's not that much of a difference between our pricing and reserving" and counterparties are doing "an independent assessment."
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: asked if casualty shrink frees capital; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "we're in a very, very strong capital position" and "I wouldn't think about the changes in our top line being directly correlated to the capital deployment."
  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: asked about growing credit/specialty mix and fee-bearing capital; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "credit is one" growth area but "those deals are lumpy"; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "we have more capital interest for the vehicles than we have opportunity to include them into the structures" and "where they're sized now is likely to be where they're sized next year."
  • Chris Hartwell, Autonomous: asked about loosening terms/conditions and cyber; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "terms and conditions remain really strong" and on cyber "we've been taking some cyber risk off the table" with "a smaller and better-protected cyber book."
  • Ryan Tunis, Cantor: asked Florida walkaways and casualty claims/rate differences by client; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: Florida exits were "mostly rate" and casualty reductions focused on cedents "less successful" in keeping up with trend.
  • Pablo Singzon, JPMorgan: asked gross vs net returns and MGA risk; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "We are still seeing a gross portfolio that's well in excess of our cost of capital" and "we are not a very large writer of MGAs"; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: in MGA E&S property, "we control the underwriting and the pricing and the cat exposure."
  • Brian Meredith, UBS: asked about alternative capital and T&Cs; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: new vehicles "haven't moved the market at this point in time"; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "Terms and conditions have stayed very strong."
  • Tracy Benguigui, Wolfe: asked retro spread and cyber/AI risk; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: "We don't disclose what the spread is" and focus is "how much we keep"; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "We haven't seen any uptick in claims due to AI or Mythos."
  • Taylor Scott, Barclays: asked reserve comfort on casualty non-renewals and Florida tort reform; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: "we do think inflation will continue"; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: Florida tort reform "actually had more impact than what that conservative original assessment anticipated" and the company was "happy to give credit for it in this year's renewal."
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI: asked about rate adequacy if declines persist and demand growth; President, CEO & Director O'Donnell: even with further declines, "we will further enhance the capital efficiency of that portfolio"; Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra: U.S. cat new demand slowed from "$20 billion" to "just over $10 billion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on market-cycle durability, casualty reserve comfort, and retro economics, with repeated questions on pricing bottoms, casualty trend, and the impact of cessions.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and disciplined, leaning on portfolio tools and capital flexibility, including "this is a margin business, not a growth business" and "we remain confident in the balance of 2026." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • Versus last quarter, management used similarly confident continuity language (strategy "does not change") while analysts shifted more heavily toward questions about where property-cat pricing "might bottom" and how long rate pressure can persist without losses.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 highlighted midyear property-cat pricing as "down high teens" and emphasized net retention discipline: "discipline is not about how much you write, it's about how much you keep." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • Q1 emphasized January renewal execution with rates "down low teen percentages" and noted property-cat top line "stayed relatively flat, excluding reinstatement premiums." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • Q2 introduced a discrete earnings item around "the settlement of the Baltimore Bridge collapse" causing a segment shift, while reiterating "the net effect on our bottom line was relatively small." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • Capital return messaging stayed consistent, with Q2 adding an in-quarter update: "So far in the third quarter through July 20, we repurchased an additional $83 million of our shares." (Executive VP & CFO Qutub)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Social inflation continues to impact casualty" and the company said it "remain[s] cautious" where uncertainty remains. (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)
  • Management flagged war risk monitoring: "The war has returned to an active phase" and "we'll continue to monitor the situation closely as facts on the ground could change rapidly." (Executive VP & Group Chief Underwriting Officer Marra)
  • Market competition and rate pressure were a recurring theme, with management expecting "continued pricing pressure" given "a lot of supply in the market." (President, CEO & Director O'Donnell)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as another quarter of strong operating profitability driven by underwriting, fee income, and record net investment income, while positioning for continued property-cat rate pressure by adjusting retention, expanding ceded protection, and maintaining share repurchases into wind season. The call also formalized a year-end CFO transition and framed casualty caution around social inflation and event-driven volatility, including the Baltimore Bridge settlement’s segment shift with a limited net impact to the quarter’s results.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tenet Healthcare Non-GAAP EPS of $6.12 beats by $1.86, revenue of $5.62B beats by $180M
data.description:
• Tenet Healthcare press release (THC): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $6.12 beats by $1.86.
  • Revenue of $5.62B (+6.6% Y/Y) beats by $180M.
  • Second quarter 2026 Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA1 increased 16.3% over second quarter 2025 to $1.304 billion; Second quarter 2026 Adjusted EBITDA margin was 23.2%; Our second quarter 2026 Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA well exceeded the high end of our second quarter guidance range of 24-25% of our previous full year guidance of $4.635 billion at the mid-point
  • Second quarter 2026 Ambulatory Care Adjusted EBITDA of $542 million increased 8.8% over second quarter 2025
  • Hospital Adjusted EBITDA margin increased to 18.0% in second quarter 2026 compared to 15.6% in second quarter 2025 despite payer mix headwinds
  • Board of Directors authorized a $2.0 billion increase to the share repurchase program
  • FY 2026 Adjusted EBITDA Outlook is now expected to be in the range of $4.83 billion to $5.03 billion, a $295 million increase at the midpoint of the range; FY 2026 Adjusted Free Cash Flow outlook now expected to be in the range of $2.725 billion to $3.025 billion, a $225 million increase

data.title: Hartford Insurance's Q2 earnings beat consensus, new stock buybacks announced
data.description:
Hartford Insurance Group (HIG) was trading lower even as the insurer announced second-quarter earnings that exceeded consensus estimates and a new $4.2B share repurchase program.

Shares were 1.59% lower at $139.93 during post-market trading on Thursday.

Core earnings per diluted share rose 6% year-over-year to $3.42, above the S&P Cap IQ consensus estimate of $3.14.

Revenue reached $7.26B from $6.72B in the same period a year ago, up from the average analyst estimate of $7.24B.

Earned premiums came in at $6.28B during Q2, while net investment income was $800M.

Property & Casualty premiums increased by 3% year-over-year, driven by Business Insurance premium growth of 5%.

The new stock buyback program, which represents a 27% increase from the prior authorization, is effective from August 1, 2026, through the end of 2028.

"Business Insurance delivered another strong quarter, with 5 percent written premium growth and an underlying combined ratio of 89.3. In personal insurance, the underlying combined ratio improved 1.7 points, while growth was impacted by a competitive market," said CFO Beth Costello.

"Employee Benefits generated fully insured ongoing premium growth of 5 percent with a core earnings margin of 7.4 percent. Investment income remained strong, supported by our diversified portfolio and attractive new money yields," said Costello.

Earnings call is scheduled for 9:00 AM ET tomorrow.
